perturbation.original_text: Any such termination shall become effective at the end of such 60-day period unless the Breaching Party has cured any such breach or default prior to the expiration of such 60-day period (or, if such default is capable of being cured but cannot be cured within such 60-day period, the Breaching Party has commenced and diligently continued actions to cure such default provided always that, in such instance, such cure must have occurred within one hundred twenty (120) days after written notice thereof was provided to the Breaching Party by the non-breaching Party to remedy such default).
perturbation.changed_text: Any such termination shall become effective at the end of such 30-day period unless the Breaching Party has cured any such breach or default prior to the expiration of such 30-day period (or, if such default is capable of being cured but cannot be cured within such 30-day period, the Breaching Party has commenced and diligently continued actions to cure such default provided always that, in such instance, such cure must have occurred within sixty (60) days after written notice thereof was provided to the Breaching Party by the non-breaching Party to remedy such default).
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the cure period for a material breach from 60 days (with a possible extension to 120 days) to 30 days (with a possible extension to 60 days).
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1161 requires a 3-day notice to cure certain breaches, but a 30 day is what is required, especially since the original states 60 days. By modifying the cure period to 30 days, the changed text could be argued as providing insufficient time to cure a breach, especially when considering the complexities of research collaboration agreements.

(Amended (as added by Stats. 2020, Ch. 37, Sec. 16) by Stats. 2024, Ch. 287, Sec. 6.)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the time allowed to cure a breach from 60 days (extendable to 120) to 30 days (extendable to 60). The Law Explanation points to California Code of Civil Procedure § 1161 which suggests a required timeframe for curing breaches, although the snippet itself only contains information about how to find the law but does not contain the law. However, the Perturbation Explanation states that there is a change to a legally required time frame, and that this contradicts the original legally sound contract. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition applies here, stating that a contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law. Therefore, the change introduces a potential violation due to insufficient time to cure, which makes the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations and prohibitions contained in this Article 10 shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations and prohibitions contained in this Article 10 shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the survival period for confidentiality obligations from 10 years to 1 year.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 339 dictates a two-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract, account, or book account, or any liability created by statute, other than a penalty or forfeiture. This includes confidentiality breaches. Modifying the survival period to 1 year could effectively limit the time to discover and act on a breach

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 339. Within two years: 1. An action upon a contract, obligation or liability not founded upon an instrument of writing, except as provided in Section 2725 of the Commercial Code or subdivision 2 of Section 337 of this code; or an action founded upon a contract, obligation or liability, evidenced by a certificate, or abstract or guaranty of title of real property, or by a policy of title insurance; provided, that the cause of action upon a contract, obligation or liability evidenced by a certificate, or abstract or guaranty of title of real property or policy of title insurance shall not be deemed to have accrued until the discovery of the loss or damage suffered by the aggrieved party thereunder. 2. An action against a sheriff or coroner upon a liability incurred by the doing of an act in an official capacity and in virtue of office, or by the omission of an official duty including the nonpayment of money collected in the enforcement of a judgment. 3. An act
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the survival period for obligations from 10 years to 1 year, potentially conflicting with the statute of limitations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CCP 339) specifies a two-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract. The changed text sets a 1-year survival period, limiting the time to discover and act on a breach. This creates a direct contradiction because the contractually agreed upon survival period is shorter than the statutory limitation period, which could effectively bar legal recourse after one year, undermining the protection intended by the statute.
